sources.notes: Statement and constraints: choose creepers to detonate; each detonation covers an interval determined by explosive power.
Official editorial confirms interval-cover DP with non-intersecting midpoint constraint and two segment-tree range-min queries per state.

annotation.summary: Choose the minimum number of detonations needed to destroy all creepers on a line, where detonating creeper i covers an interval centered at i and selected detonation centers must be compatible with killing order.
annotation.constraints: sum n <= 500000; a quadratic interval DP is too slow, requiring O(n log n) optimization.
annotation.core_idea: Model each detonated creeper i as an interval [i-e_i+1, i+e_i-1]. Unlike standard interval covering, two selected ranges cannot have intersecting midpoints, because detonating one first would kill the other. Define dp[i] as the best cost when creeper i is the rightmost detonated creeper and covers through i+e_i-1. The possible predecessor lies in one of two interval relation cases. Processing i from left to right, a segment tree stores the best cost for already reachable covered prefixes, so dp[i] is the minimum of two range-min queries; then update the endpoint i+e_i-1 with dp[i].
annotation.complexity: O(n log n) per test case
tricks: Turn each detonation into a coverage interval.
Account for the non-intersecting-midpoints constraint between selected detonations.
Optimize prefix-coverage DP transitions with segment-tree range minima.

solution_variants.name: interval-dp-segment-tree
solution_variants.summary: Precompute each detonation interval, use DP over the rightmost selected center, and answer predecessor transitions with a segment tree.

solution_variants.name: quadratic-interval-dp
solution_variants.summary: Directly enumerate the previous detonated center and current center to understand the two predecessor cases.

tags.evidence: The minimum detonation count is computed by states for the rightmost chosen center and the prefix it covers.
The optimized solution needs range-min queries over predecessor coverage states and point minimum updates.
Transition validity is characterized by how the current interval and predecessor coverage interval overlap or touch.
The natural processing order is by position; no comparison sorting is central to the algorithm.
